statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer which is the number of nodes in the linked list. This is followed by the data of each node in the linked list, one per line. The last line contains -1 to indicate the end of the linked list.
statement.outputFormat: The data of each node in the linked list, one per line.

constraints.description: number of nodes in the linked list

examples.explanation: The linked list is 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5. The data of each node is printed.
The linked list is 10 -> 20 -> 30. The data of each node is printed.

hints.text: To start solving this problem, you should first understand the structure of a singly linked list and how to traverse it. A singly linked list consists of nodes, where each node contains some data and a reference (i.e., a link) to the next node in the sequence.

Given the head node of the linked list, you can start traversing the list by following the next pointer of each node. You should continue this process until you reach a node whose next pointer is null, indicating the end of the list.

To print the data of each node, you can use a loop that iterates over the nodes in the linked list. Inside the loop, you can access the data of the current node and print it. Then, you can move to the next node by updating the current node to its next node.

editorial.title: Printing a Singly Linked List
editorial.intuition: The problem requires printing the data stored in each node of a singly linked list. The intuition is to traverse the linked list from the head node to the last node, printing the data of each node.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve recursively or iteratively traversing the linked list, printing the data of each node. However, this approach is already optimal with a time complexity of O(N), where N is the number of nodes in the linked list.
editorial.betterSolution: No better solution exists as the current approach has a time complexity of O(N) which is the minimum required to print all nodes in the linked list.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves initializing a pointer to the head of the linked list and then traversing the list until the pointer becomes None, printing the data of each node. This approach has a time complexity of O(N) and a space complexity of O(1) as it only uses a constant amount of space.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness lies in the fact that the solution traverses the linked list from the head node to the last node, printing the data of each node exactly once. This ensures that all nodes in the linked list are printed.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution can be performed by creating a sample linked list and then printing the data of each node using the provided solution.
commonMistakes: Not checking for the existence of the head node before traversing the linked list
Not updating the pointer to the next node after printing the data of the current node
Using recursion instead of iteration, which can lead to a stack overflow for large linked lists
Not handling the case where the linked list is empty
Not consuming the -1 input after reading the linked list
